serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant or home serving of creamy chicken pasta is approximately 300 g, equivalent to a single-plate meal portion.

nutrient_source.title: Creamy Chicken Pasta — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05060036 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from the cream and butter component profile as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for creamy chicken pasta as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 based on the GI of cooked white pasta (GI ~45–50, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. The cream and chicken components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are expected to lower the effective GI of the dish relative to plain pasta. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 35.1) / 100 = 15.8 g, using a 300 g serving containing 11.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g (= 35.1 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 244 mg per 100 g; cumulative sodium in a 300 g serving reaches approximately 732 mg, representing about one-third of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, avoid adding extra salt or Parmesan, and pair the dish with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 11.7 g per 100 g with a low estimated GI of 45; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 35 g of carbohydrate, which is within a single-meal carbohydrate budget for many type 2 diabetes management plans, but portion discipline is essential.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to 200–250 g, choose whole-wheat pasta to increase dietary fibre above 3 g per 100 g for improved glycaemic control, and monitor post-meal blood glucose as cumulative pasta carbohydrate can be significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 8.81 g per 100 g with estimated saturated fat approximately 4.5 g per 100 g; a 300 g serving contributes roughly 13.5 g saturated fat, approaching the 14–20 g daily saturated fat ceiling recommended for cardiovascular risk reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should reduce cream quantity or substitute with low-fat evaporated milk, use olive oil in place of butter, limit serving size to 200 g, and increase vegetable content to improve the overall dietary fat profile of the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Creamy chicken pasta is a Western-style pasta dish consisting of cooked pasta — typically penne, fettuccine, or spaghetti — tossed with diced or sliced chicken pieces in a rich white cream sauce. The sauce is made from heavy cream, butter, garlic, onion, and often finished with grated Parmesan cheese, salt, black pepper, and fresh parsley. The dish has a smooth, velvety texture with a savoury, mildly rich flavour. It is widely served in casual dining restaurants, cafeterias, and home kitchens across Singapore, Malaysia, and other parts of Southeast Asia as a working-lunch or dinner option. Per 100 g, the dish provides 159 kcal, 8.3 g protein, 8.81 g fat, and 11.7 g carbohydrate, with 244 mg sodium and 1.2 g dietary fibre. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 45 (low), based on the dominant carbohydrate contribution from cooked white pasta. Clinically, the dish is moderate in sodium and saturated fat, requiring portion control for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The high protein content from chicken helps moderate the glycaemic response. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and dairy ingredients.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95 in the input record. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from ingredient composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish, which introduces minor uncertainty but does not affect the high confidence classification for the core macros.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from the cream and butter component profile of the dish, consistent with the health_suitability notes in the input record. HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish entry. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite creamy chicken pasta dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cr_id F05060036 as of 2026-04-16, and no sufficiently close equivalent entry was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy to supply these values without introducing unacceptable estimation uncertainty: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16) does not publish these sub-nutrient fields for composite mixed dishes. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy was also reviewed; no exact match for creamy chicken pasta as a composite dish exists, and component-level disaggregation was not performed to avoid compounding estimation error. These fields are therefore set to -1 (data not available) per the Veritas numeric hygiene rule. 3. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white pasta GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for creamy chicken pasta as a composite dish is available in the approved GI source. 4. This is a Western-style dish with no mandatory Singaporean multilingual naming requirement under the G6 rule; local_names fields are null accordingly. The dish is consumed in Singapore and Malaysia but originates from Western culinary tradition. 5. Halal dietary flag is set to true consistent with the input record dietary_tags; this assumes halal-certified chicken and dairy are used in preparation.
